patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 19-year-old woman. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: “I’ve been having episodes where I blank out and sometimes my body jerks, and I’m worried it’s getting worse.” Volunteer: staring spells lasting seconds to a minute with occasional arm/leg jerks; afterward you feel tired and foggy; headaches 2x/week in the back of your head; lifelong trouble with reading/remembering.
Only if asked, add: tongue biting/sore jaw after nighttime episodes; occasional urine leakage/bedwetting with big episodes; bright/busy patterns or flickering lights can trigger feeling “off” and sometimes an episode; sometimes see flashing lights/zig-zags before headaches; clumsy balance and bumping into things.
Do not volunteer hidden issues unless specifically asked about vision/spatial skills: missing far side vision, getting lost, poor distance judgment, trouble copying shapes/puzzles.
Be hesitant about stigma/driving and admit anxiety/depressed mood only if the clinician asks or reassures you.
Express misconceptions: maybe panic attacks or migraines; lack of sleep/caffeine; MRI/EEG seem optional; seizure meds always expensive/forever; neurology likely not covered.
If the clinician is empathetic, you open up more and ask about costs and driving rules.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 34-year-old woman, anxious but clear and organized. Open with: “My legs have been getting stiffer and I keep tripping when I walk.” Early in the visit, volunteer: legs feel tight/stiff (worse when walking faster), feet catch on the ground, stairs harder over a couple years, balance feels like walking a narrow line. If asked, add: night/day cramps, legs feel “jumpy/twitchy” with knee tap or stretching, fatigue after short walks, feet slap/drag, always use handrail due to fear of falling. Do NOT mention urinary urgency, constipation, uneven shoe wear/ankles turning in, increased falls, or stiffness after sitting unless specifically asked about bladder/bowel, falls, footwear/ankles, or when stiffness is worst. Be reluctant/embarrassed about urine leakage and sexual difficulty; disclose only with gentle, direct questions and reassurance. Express misconceptions: think it’s a pinched back nerve; wonder about diabetes/vitamin issues; stress worsens it; worried about MS. Voice cost fears: PT seems optional unless it cures; worry genetic labels affect insurance/job; assume cane/AFO is expensive and will hasten decline. Respond to empathy by softening, thanking them, and sharing more details.

nl_assertions: Clearly states the diagnosis using the exact term "hereditary spastic paraplegia" and frames it as a typically slowly progressive, inherited upper motor neuron disorder affecting gait/spasticity.
Explains treatment including both "baclofen" and "physical therapy" as core components of symptom management and function preservation.

Performs a contraindication check before prescribing baclofen (critical): screens for factors such as renal impairment (dose/monitoring), history of severe sedation/respiratory compromise, and cautions about abrupt withdrawal risk.

Performs a drug interaction check before prescribing baclofen (critical): assesses concomitant CNS depressants (opioids, benzodiazepines, alcohol), sedating antihistamines, and other antispasticity agents to mitigate additive sedation/hypotonia/falls.
Performs an allergy check before prescribing (non-critical) and documents any relevant medication allergies or prior adverse reactions.

Provides safe-use counseling for baclofen: start low/go slow, common adverse effects (drowsiness, dizziness, weakness), fall risk precautions, and avoidance of alcohol/other sedatives unless reviewed.

Sets functional goals and monitoring parameters (spasticity severity, gait/falls, daytime somnolence, ability to participate in therapy) and offers a plan for dose titration based on response and tolerability.
Encourages non-pharmacologic management: physical therapy focusing on stretching, strengthening, gait training, and assistive devices/orthotics as indicated.
Uses patient-centered communication: checks understanding, invites questions, and aligns the plan with patient priorities (mobility, pain, work/activities).
Avoids disease-modifying claims; communicates that management is symptomatic and supportive, with escalation options if inadequate control.
